title: Bombay potato
subtitle: Dry-spiced potatoes with mustard seeds and turmeric
excerpt: Cooked potato cubes tossed in a spice base of mustard seeds, turmeric, cumin, and chilli until the spices coat the potato surface and the edges begin to crisp. A dry side dish with no sauce, the potatoes are the vehicle for the spice coating. One of the most widely served side dishes on the British Indian menu.
sourceNotes: Bombay aloo (potatoes cooked with Indian spices) is a standard side dish across British Indian restaurants and home cooking. The Mumbai connection in the name reflects the potato's historic importance as a Bombay street food item, though the British curry-house version is a simplified adaptation.

content.text: Bombay potato is defined by the texture of the potato surface, the spice coating should be dry and slightly crisped onto the potato, not a sauce. This means the potatoes must be fully cooked before they go into the spiced oil, and the pan must be hot enough to dry and colour the exterior in the short time the potatoes are in the pan. A heavy pan on medium-high heat, with the potatoes not moved too frequently, achieves this.

Mustard seeds are the signature flavour element. They must pop before the other spices are added, unpopped mustard seeds are bitter and dense; popped seeds are nutty and aromatic. Have the remaining ingredients ready before the seeds go into the hot oil, as the sequence moves quickly.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: peeled and cut into 2 cm cubes

content.text: Cook the potatoes and apply the spice coating

Boil the {{potato}} cubes in well-salted water for 8 to 10 minutes until just tender. Drain and leave to steam-dry for 5 minutes. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide heavy pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{mustard-seeds-black}} and {{cumin-seeds}} and cook for 30 to 40 seconds until the mustard seeds pop. Add the {{garlic}} and {{ginger-root}} and stir for 30 seconds. Add the {{turmeric}}, {{chilli-powder}}, and {{coriander-ground}} and stir for 20 seconds. Add the drained potatoes and toss to coat. Cook for 8 to 10 minutes, stirring occasionally and allowing the potatoes to sit undisturbed between stirs so the surface crisps. Season with salt, stir in the {{garam-masala}}, and finish with lemon juice and coriander if using.

items.fix: Steam-dry the potatoes thoroughly after boiling. A hot pan is essential, the spice mixture should sizzle on contact with the potato surface
items.cause: Potatoes too wet or pan not hot enough

items.fix: Let the potatoes sit undisturbed for 2 to 3 minutes before stirring. They will release cleanly once the surface has crisped
items.cause: Moved too soon after adding to the spiced oil

items.fix: The turmeric and cumin must be fried in the oil for at least 20 seconds before the potatoes are added. Stir-frying the spices in the oil blooms their flavour
items.cause: Insufficient salt or spices not bloomed

items.fix: Pull the potatoes at just-tender. They should hold their shape when a knife point is inserted. They will break down further in the pan

content.text: Bombay potato is a steady curry-house side. It sits in a different spot from saag aloo. Saag aloo has spinach as a partner; Bombay potato is all potato with the spice coating as the point. It is one of the few curry sides that works as well cool as it does hot. That makes it useful at a buffet or a family-style table. The mustard seed and turmeric mix is the flavour that gives it away the moment it lands on the table.
